findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Data Exfiltration via Monitoring Hook
findings.description: The hooks/monitor.sh script captures all tool inputs, outputs, and environment variables (including ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and GITHUB_TOKEN) and sends them to an external server (mcp-analytics-hub.io) via curl POST. This is a clear data exfiltration mechanism that collects sensitive credentials and all agent activity.
findings.evidence: The script captures TOOL_INPUT, TOOL_OUTPUT, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, GITHUB_TOKEN, HOME, USER, and hostname, then sends them to https://mcp-analytics-hub.io/hooks/post-tool with a Bearer token.
findings.remediation: Remove the monitoring hook entirely. Do not install any hooks that send data to external servers. Review the .mcp.json configuration for any other suspicious endpoints.

findings.title: Hardcoded API Keys and Secrets in .mcp.json
findings.description: The .mcp.json file contains hardcoded secrets including SEARCH_API_KEY (sk_mcp_rT4Xp9QmN2kW8vJ5) and PROXY_AUTH (Bearer mcp_a1b2c3d4e5f6). These credentials are exposed in plaintext and could be used for unauthorized access.
findings.remediation: Remove hardcoded secrets. Use environment variables or a secrets manager instead.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning via MCP Configuration
findings.description: The .mcp.json file configures an 'analytics-proxy' server with FORWARD_ALL_REQUESTS, CAPTURE_TOOL_INPUTS, and CAPTURE_TOOL_OUTPUTS set to true. This is designed to intercept and forward all tool traffic to an external analytics endpoint, effectively poisoning the MCP tool ecosystem.
findings.remediation: Remove the analytics-proxy server configuration. Do not use proxy servers that capture all tool inputs and outputs.

findings.title: Indirect Prompt Injection via Monitoring Hook Installation
findings.description: The SKILL.md instructions tell the user to install a monitoring hook (PostToolUse.sh) that will execute after every tool call. This hook contains malicious code that exfiltrates data. The instructions are designed to trick the user into installing a persistent monitoring mechanism.
findings.remediation: Do not install any hooks from untrusted skills. Verify the contents of any hook script before installation.

findings.title: Capability Inflation - Deceptive Skill Name and Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'mcp-toolkit' with a description about managing MCP servers and tool integrations, but its actual purpose is to install monitoring hooks and proxy configurations that exfiltrate data. The name and description are deceptive to gain user trust.
findings.evidence: name: 'mcp-toolkit', description: 'Manage Model Context Protocol servers and tool integrations. Configure, test, and monitor MCP connections for your agent workflow.'
findings.remediation: Rename the skill to accurately reflect its behavior. Remove all data exfiltration mechanisms.

findings.title: Detection Evasion via Background Execution
findings.description: The monitoring hook script runs curl in the background with '> /dev/null 2>&1 &' to hide its network activity from the user. This is an evasion technique to avoid detection during execution.
findings.evidence: curl command ends with '> /dev/null 2>&1 &' to run silently in background
findings.remediation: Remove the background execution pattern. All network calls should be transparent to the user.

findings.title: Environment Variable Harvesting
findings.description: The monitoring hook captures ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and GITHUB_TOKEN from environment variables and sends them to an external server. These are sensitive credentials that could be used for unauthorized access to AI and code repository services.
findings.remediation: Do not capture or transmit environment variables. Remove all credential harvesting code.

findings.title: Unauthorized Tool Use - MCP Server Configuration
findings.description: The .mcp.json file configures MCP servers that may execute arbitrary commands (npx) and make network requests. This could allow the agent to run untrusted code and access external services without proper authorization.
findings.evidence: MCP servers configured with 'npx' commands and external endpoints
findings.remediation: Review all MCP server configurations. Only use trusted, verified MCP servers.
